summary:
Key indicators include being afebrile with normalizing platelet counts post-shock recovery; however, returning appetite isn’t as definitive an indicator ensuring full safety from complications prior to discharge.
The presence of returned appetite while important may be insufficient by itself when considering other critical parameters such as temperature stability and normalized blood cell levels essential during DHF management leading towards safer release conditions. Agent2 reasoned through each option based primarily around established medical guidelines related to managing patients diagnosed with Dengue Haemorrhagic Fever focusing particularly on core aspects including body temperatures normalization, hematological improvements especially concerning platelet counts alongside signs indicating overall systemic health status beyond just dietary changes which could occur independently even if underlying issues persist requiring continued care under professional supervision until all risk factors have been adequately addressed according to current best practices within infectious diseases treatment protocols specifically tailored toward this condition type. 

In summary:

Key indicators ensure stable vital signs, proper laboratory values reflecting reduced infection activity, but reappearing hunger might still imply residual symptoms necessitating further observation rather than immediate clearance back into community settings where potential exposures remain possible thus underscoring why solely relying upon increased food intake wouldn't suffice fully addressing comprehensive readiness required pre-discharge evaluation procedures mandated per standard operating instructions governing similar cases involving severe forms of viral illnesses characterized
